text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Patient: Robert Delacroix
DOB: [on file]
MRN: [on file]
Attending: [Attending Oncologist]
Department: Genitourinary Medical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Return visit for follow-up of metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), 2 months after initiation of enzalutamide (Xtandi). PSA check, toxicity assessment, and disease monitoring.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Delacroix is a 70-year-old French-Canadian male with a well-established history of Stage IVB (cT3bN1M1b) prostatic adenocarcinoma, Gleason 9 (4+5), Grade Group 5, diagnosed approximately 2 years ago when his PSA was 87.4 ng/mL with bone metastases in the pelvis, lumbar spine, and right femur at presentation. He initially received ADT with leuprolide (Lupron) 22.5 mg IM every 3 months in combination with abiraterone acetate (Zytiga) 1000 mg daily plus prednisone 5 mg BID, achieving a PSA nadir of 0.8 ng/mL at 9 months. He had been on ADT + Zytiga for approximately 22 months total. Over the past 3 months prior to today, his PSA rose sequentially from a nadir of 0.8 ng/mL to 1.8, then 3.4, then 6.7 ng/mL while testosterone remained confirmed castrate (most recently 9 ng/dL), thus satisfying CRPC criteria by PCWG3 definition. Abiraterone was discontinued at the prior visit. Given his known germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT, confirmed heterozygous with somatic LOH in tumor) and the potential to reserve olaparib (Lynparza) for a later line when radiographic progression occurs, the multidisciplinary decision was to initiate enzalutamide as the next agent. He was started on Xtandi 160 mg orally once daily approximately 8 weeks ago. ADT with leuprolide and bone-protective therapy with zoledronic acid (Reclast) 4 mg IV every 3 months were continued.

Today he presents for his first on-treatment follow-up since starting Xtandi. PSA has been checked at an outside laboratory and results are available. He reports several new or worsening symptoms since starting treatment.

He notes fatigue that he grades as moderately bothersome - able to complete activities of daily living independently but has cut back significantly on his usual walks and light household tasks. He describes this as more pronounced than what he experienced on Zytiga. He also notes intermittent dizziness and lightheadedness, particularly when rising from sitting or lying down. He denies any syncopal episodes. Hot flashes have worsened substantially - he describes them as very frequent (every 1-2 hours), drenching at night, significantly disrupting sleep. He was previously managing on Zytiga with grade 2 hot flashes but now finds these to be significantly more disruptive.

Of note, his wife reports that he has had two near-fall episodes in the past month, once stepping off a curb and once in the bathroom. He did not actually fall and had no injury but expresses concern about this. No history of prior falls. He denies any new focal neurologic symptoms, chest pain, or palpitations. Appetite is maintained. No new back pain. Bowel and bladder function unchanged (he has mild baseline urinary frequency related to prior prostate disease). He is compliant with medications and denies missing any doses.

His PDE-5 inhibitor (sildenafil, which he had been using occasionally) was discontinued at the prior visit given drug interaction concerns and CNS risk profile with enzalutamide.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive: Fatigue (grade 2), dizziness/lightheadedness with positional changes (grade 1), severe hot flashes with night sweats (grade 3), near-fall episodes x2 (no injury), mild nocturia x2-3 per night (baseline), decreased exercise tolerance.
Negative: No chest pain, no shortness of breath at rest, no palpitations, no headaches, no new cognitive changes beyond mild foggy thinking he has had for some time on ADT (no acute worsening), no limb weakness or numbness, no visual changes, no nausea or vomiting, no abdominal pain, no new bone pain, no hematuria, no lower extremity swelling worsening.

---

PHYSICAL EXAM
General: Well-appearing male, in no acute distress. Alert and fully oriented. Slightly fatigued-appearing.
Vitals: BP 138/82 mmHg (sitting), HR 72 bpm, RR 14, Temp 36.8 C, SpO2 98% on room air. Weight: 91 kg (up ~2 kg from prior visit, which may reflect fluid retention vs. weight from reduced activity). BMI 28.4.
Orthostatics performed: Supine BP 138/82; standing at 1 minute 122/74 (drop of 16 mmHg systolic) - borderline orthostatic; no symptomatic drop at this measurement but patient notes this is when he feels lightheaded.
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: PERRL, no icterus, no lymphadenopathy in the cervical chains.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated.
Musculoskeletal: No focal bone tenderness over the spine on palpation. No new back pain elicited with percussion over thoracic or lumbar spine.
Extremities: Trace bilateral lower extremity edema (1+, pitting). Pulses 2+ bilaterally.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal motor or sensory deficits. Gait assessment: mildly unsteady on tandem gait testing. Romberg negative but he requires a moment to stabilize. No tremor. Cognitive screen (brief): oriented x3, recalls 3/3 objects at 5 minutes.
Skin: No rashes. No gynecomastia noted (breast exam normal).

---

RELEVANT LABORATORY RESULTS
PSA (today): 2.1 ng/mL (down from 6.7 ng/mL at time of enzalutamide initiation; 69% decline)
Testosterone: 12 ng/dL (confirmed castrate, <50 ng/dL threshold)
CBC: WBC 6.8 x10^9/L, Hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL, Hematocrit 37.8%, Platelets 214 x10^9/L. No significant cytopenias at this time.
Comprehensive metabolic panel: Sodium 140, Potassium 4.1, Creatinine 1.05 mg/dL (eGFR ~68 mL/min/1.73m2), BUN 18, Glucose 102, ALT 32 U/L, AST 28 U/L, Total Bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, Alkaline Phosphatase 124 U/L (mildly elevated, chronic from bone disease, slightly improved from prior). LDH 188 U/L.
Lipid panel (checked given ongoing ADT/cardiovascular risk): LDL 98 mg/dL, HDL 42 mg/dL, Triglycerides 162 mg/dL.
HbA1C: 5.9% (borderline, consistent with metabolic effects of long-term ADT/prednisone exposure; prednisone now discontinued with abiraterone).

---

IMAGING
No new imaging reviewed today. Bone scan performed at 18 months on prior abiraterone-based therapy showed stable osseous metastases in lumbar spine, pelvis, and right femur without new lesions. CT C/A/P performed earlier while on abiraterone showed no visceral disease. Repeat imaging not yet indicated given he is only 2 months into current therapy. Will plan restaging CT and bone scan at approximately 4-6 months on enzalutamide.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. METASTATIC CASTRATION-RESISTANT PROSTATE CANCER (mCRPC), post-abiraterone, now 2 months on enzalutamide (Xtandi 160 mg daily):
Excellent early PSA response - PSA has declined from 6.7 to 2.1 ng/mL, a 69% decline at 8 weeks. This is an encouraging early response signal. He is AR-positive and BRCA2-mutant, but also harbors multiple co-alterations (TP53, PTEN loss, CDK12 biallelic, RB1) on prior NGS, which can limit duration of ARSI benefit. Will continue enzalutamide at current dose of 160 mg daily. Continue leuprolide (Lupron Depot) 22.5 mg IM - next injection due in 4 weeks, confirm scheduling. Continue zoledronic acid 4 mg IV quarterly for bone-protective therapy; calcium 1200 mg + vitamin D 1000 IU daily ongoing. Recheck PSA in 8 weeks. Plan CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and bone scan at approximately 4 months on therapy.

2. FATIGUE (Grade 2, likely multifactorial - enzalutamide-related CNS effect, ADT-related, anemia mild):
Hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL, so anemia is not a major contributor at this time. Fatigue on enzalutamide is well-recognized and can be CNS-mediated given its blood-brain barrier penetration. No dose reduction warranted at this time given it is grade 2 and he remains ECOG PS 1. Encouraged graded aerobic exercise as tolerated - evidence supports reduced fatigue with exercise in this population. Physical therapy referral placed (see #4). Counseled that fatigue may improve with time on therapy. If grade 3 or significantly affecting ADLs, will reassess dose.

3. DIZZINESS / LIGHTHEADEDNESS (Grade 1, orthostatic pattern):
Orthostatics today show a borderline 16 mmHg systolic drop without symptoms at rest measurement, consistent with mild orthostasis. Enzalutamide can contribute to this. Counseled on orthostatic precautions: rise slowly, dangle legs at bedside before standing, maintain adequate hydration. Ensure he is not over-treated for hypertension - will hold on adding additional antihypertensives given borderline orthostasis. BP was 138/82 today which is acceptable without aggressive treatment. He is not on any antihypertensives currently. No dose reduction of enzalutamide indicated at this time given grade 1 toxicity.

4. FALLS RISK (Near-falls x2 since starting enzalutamide):
Enzalutamide carries a known risk of falls and fractures, partly from CNS effects (dizziness, fatigue) and partly from ADT-related muscle loss, balance impairment, and bone loss. Tandem gait mildly unsteady on exam today. Formal physical therapy referral placed for fall prevention program, balance training, and home safety assessment. Discussed fall prevention with patient and his wife: remove trip hazards, install bathroom grab bars, non-slip mats. Encouraged him to use a cane or walking support if needed. Denosumab vs. continuation of zoledronic acid was considered in this context - will continue zoledronic acid for now given established bone metastases, and optimize vitamin D. Vitamin D 25-OH level ordered to ensure adequate levels.

5. HOT FLASHES (Grade 3, severe, disrupting sleep):
Worsened substantially on enzalutamide compared to prior abiraterone-based therapy. Significantly affecting quality of life and sleep. Will initiate venlafaxine 37.5 mg orally daily for 1 week then increase to 75 mg daily as tolerated for hot flash management (evidence-based, preferred over medroxyprogesterone in this population, avoids hormone concerns). Counseled on sleep hygiene, cooling strategies. If inadequate response, can consider gabapentin at night. Cyproterone is not appropriate here.

6. GERMLINE BRCA2 MUTATION / PARP INHIBITOR STRATEGY:
As discussed previously, given germline BRCA2 p.Ser1982Argfs*22, patient remains eligible for olaparib (Lynparza) therapy. Strategy remains to continue enzalutamide while PSA response is maintained, reserving olaparib for subsequent line, particularly if/when radiographic progression occurs. This plan was discussed with patient and he understands. Will reassess at the time of progression.

7. CARDIOVASCULAR RISK / METABOLIC:
Long-term ADT associated with metabolic syndrome risk. HbA1C 5.9% - borderline pre-diabetic range, consistent with metabolic effects; continue to monitor, dietary counseling provided. LDL 98 mg/dL - acceptable. Triglycerides 162 mg/dL - mildly elevated, dietary modifications discussed. No new antihypertensive needed at this time given orthostasis concern (BP 138/82 today).

8. BONE HEALTH / ZOLEDRONIC ACID:
Continue zoledronic acid quarterly. Dental clearance previously obtained. Remind patient to maintain good oral hygiene and avoid invasive dental procedures without oncology/dental team communication (ONJ precaution). Vitamin D level being checked.

RETURN VISIT: 8 weeks for PSA recheck and clinical assessment. If symptoms worsen significantly prior to that (new neurologic symptoms, significant fall, acute pain), patient instructed to call the oncology nurse line.

Time spent with patient and family: 45 minutes. Greater than 50% of the visit time was spent in counseling and coordination of care.

[Electronically signed by Attending Genitourinary Oncologist]
